one_line_takeaway: An empirical, bibliometric survey of 383 peer-reviewed AI-safety papers showing the field is overwhelmingly about concrete near-term technical risks (noise/outliers, monitoring, robustness) rather than existential risk, used to argue for framing AI safety as mainstream technological/systems safety; it is a field-mapping position paper, not a security contribution.
key_finding: Peer-reviewed AI-safety research is dominated by concrete, near-term, technical concerns rather than existential risk. Among eight risk types, the largest is noise and outliers (110 papers, 23.2%), followed by lack of monitoring (72, 15.2%) and system misspecification (66, 13.9%); adversarial attack is only 10.3% (49 papers) and explicitly existential/'undesirable behaviour' work is the smallest at 20 papers (4.2%). Methodologically, applied algorithms dominate (115 papers, 30.0%) and simulated agents follow (86, 22.5%), while purely philosophical work is 3.9% and literature reviews 3.1%. The VOSViewer co-occurrence analysis surfaces four coherent clusters (human/society-centric motivations; safe reinforcement learning; supervised-learning robustness; adversarial attacks & defense). The authors read this as evidence that AI safety is a broad, technically grounded engineering field continuous with aviation, medical, control-theory and cyber safety, and argue the existential-risk-centric framing is empirically unrepresentative and potentially exclusionary.
core_contribution: A systematic literature review (following Kitchenham & Charters guidelines plus Wohlin snowball sampling) of 383 primarily peer-reviewed AI-safety papers, querying Web of Science and Scopus (finalized 1 Nov 2023). Its own contribution is a bibliometric/empirical mapping of the field along two axes: (RQ1) what categories of safety risk are addressed across the AI system lifecycle, and (RQ2) what mitigation methodologies are proposed. On the basis of this empirical map the authors advance a position/argument: that AI safety should be understood as an epistemically inclusive, pluralistic field and a natural extension of traditional technological/systems safety engineering, rather than being framed primarily around existential risk from advanced AI. The empirical map (not the argument) is the novel deliverable.
threat_model: N/A. This is a science-of-the-field survey and position paper; it has no threat model, adversary, or defended system of its own. It catalogues risk types found in OTHER people's papers (e.g. adversarial attacks are one of eight risk categories it counts, at 10.3% of papers), but proposes no attack, defense, or security mechanism itself. No threat model should be inferred for it.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) a one-off review captures only the state at query time (1 Nov 2023) and misses more recent work, including industry outputs from Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.; (2) restriction to peer-reviewed literature excludes much of the field — arXiv preprints are often the first outlet for AI-safety work — only partially mitigated by snowball sampling; (3) the authors' own keyword/code annotation introduces annotator bias (they argue it is minimal across 383 papers); (4) the review excludes non-peer-reviewed venues central to some safety communities (LessWrong, AI Alignment Forum). Additional reviewer notes: the risk taxonomy is inherited from a single blog-post source (DeepMind [67]); paper counts are keyword/coding-dependent and papers can fall into multiple categories (categories overlap and do not sum cleanly); and the central claim (that existential framing is disproportionate) is partly an artifact of the peer-reviewed-only inclusion criterion, since much existential-risk discourse lives in exactly the non-peer-reviewed venues excluded — a point the authors acknowledge.

datasets_used: Corpus of 383 primarily peer-reviewed AI-safety papers (the study's own analysis dataset), selected from Web of Science and Scopus indexing databases
2,666 de-duplicated papers from the database query (pre-screening) plus 117 papers from snowball sampling seeded by 11 highly-influential papers
Google Scholar citation counts (as of 27 Jan 2024) recorded per paper as metadata; no machine-learning benchmark datasets are used (this is a meta-study)
quantitative_results: Final analyzed corpus: 383 papers; database search 2,666, snowball 117, query finalized 1 Nov 2023

Risk types (RQ1, Fig 3): noise & outliers 110 (23.2%); lack of monitoring 72 (15.2%); system misspecification 66 (13.9%); lack of control enforcement 57 (12.0%); unsafe exploration 54 (11.4%); adversarial attack 49 (10.3%); non-stationary distribution 47 (9.9%); undesirable behaviour 20 (4.2%)

Methodology categories (RQ2, Fig 6): applied algorithm 115 (30.0%); simulated agents 86 (22.5%); analysis framework 48 (12.5%); theoretical algorithm 34 (8.9%); theoretical framework 24 (6.3%); design framework 21 (5.5%); mechanistic interpretability 21 (5.5%); philosophical 15 (3.9%); literature review 12 (3.1%); real-world testing 7 (1.8%)
Theoretical vs applied split (Fig 5): Theoretical/No-Evaluation = 49 without algorithm + 15 with algorithm; Applied/Evaluated = 25 without algorithm + 102 with algorithm (categories overlap)
Publication growth (Fig 4), papers per year 2015-2023: 12, 10, 10, 21, 41, 61, 65, 104, 117; marked growth since 2016

Term co-occurrence analysis (VOSViewer, min term frequency 10, relevance >=60%) yields 4 clusters: human/society motivations (red), safe RL (blue), supervised-learning classification robustness (green), adversarial attacks & defense (yellow)
baselines_compared: None — no experimental baselines; this is a systematic literature review, not an empirical method with comparators

Methodological reference points rather than baselines: DeepMind risk categorization [67] as the basis for the 8-type risk taxonomy; Kitchenham & Charters [31] systematic-review guidelines; Wohlin [32] snowballing guidelines; prior near-term-vs-existential dichotomy framings [29, 30] which the paper argues against
